text: The laparoscopic view shows a large anterior myoma on the uterine wall and multiple small subserous myomas. A uterine manipulator is used to reveal additional myomas on the posterior wall. A myoma from the right iliac fossa is pulled towards the upper abdomen, exposing its pedicle. Vasopressin is injected to induce transient ischemia, and a harmonic scalpel is used to cut the pedicle of a large pedunculated myoma attached to the posterior uterine wall. The harmonic scalpel operates at a minimum setting, slowly dissecting the myoma from the uterus. Two pedunculated myomas, one large and one small, are detached. A bipolar device is applied to the base of a third myoma, which is then cut with the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon deposits removed myomas into the cul-de-sac, ensuring accurate counting. Vasopressin is injected into the anterior wall myoma, visible as a jet from puncture sites, inducing transient ischemia. The harmonic device cuts the serous layer of the anterior wall myoma, followed by the introduction of a myoma screw for anterosuperior traction. The myoma is slowly enucleated, revealing a clear tissue plane, and separated as the fourth myoma.

The surgeon separates the fifth pedunculated subserous myoma and places it into the cul-de-sac. The sixth myoma is also separated. Suction irrigation is performed, followed by suturing of the anterior wall of the uterus using a Y-Grill and a 30 mm needle. A deep bite is taken with a tumble square knot to approximate the tissue under tension.

The surgeon performs suturing on the uterine wall following myomectomy, using a tumble square knot technique. A single wrap is taken, followed by a loose square knot, which is tightened using the tip of a Maryland instrument to ensure hemostasis. Continuous suturing is applied, with additional locking knots to secure the tissue and prevent bleeding. The suturing is done carefully to maintain uterine wall integrity, especially in subserous myomas.

The surgeon performs ipsilateral suturing with a tumbled square knot technique, tightening the knot using a Maryland dissector. Suction irrigation is used to assess the surgical field, revealing serosal folds. A seventh myoma is identified on the posterior wall of the uterus and is separated for removal.

The surgeon carefully pulls out the myoma using a myoma screw, emphasizing the importance of maintaining a good dissection plane. Blunt dissection is performed to separate the myoma from surrounding tissues. The posterior uterine wall is sutured, ensuring that the sutures do not damage the fallopian tube, as the patient is nulliparous and needs to preserve fertility. An instrument is used to lift the uterus during suturing, and a resurgence knot is tied using a Maryland forceps.

The surgeon performs suturing on the uterine wall, starting with a loose knot and taking a second bite to address visible bleeding. The assistant lifts the structure for better access, and the knots are tightened to secure the tissue. Minimal bleeding is observed, which will be managed with electrocautery, avoiding excessive current use.

The surgeon uses a Maryland instrument to tighten the suture, followed by trimming the suture ends. Thorough suction and irrigation are performed to ensure no bleeding remains. The uterus is inspected, confirming no visible myomas. The final step involves careful morselation of the myoma, with the morselator's ear visible during the process.
